title: Shannon Rumsey
text: My name is Shannon and I am an Undergraduate Researcher at the University of California, Santa Barbara. While I am well-versed in the world of data science, my specialty is in computational linguistics and natural language processing.

title: about me
text:
During my undergraduate studies at UCSB, I had the opportunity to work on my first research paper titled Identifying Optimal Case Onset Points for Early Detection of Influenza-like Illness. For this project, I worked alongside a group of 4 and a data science team at Evidation Health Inc. for 6 months. At the end, I participated in two project showcases, one on campus and another at the KDD 2023 conference in Long Beach.








KDD 2023 conference in Long Beach.

While working on my first research project, I was sponsored by the NSF to promote the field of data science within my community. As part of my job, I curated numerous event posters and aided a professor with one of their course websites.
Currently, I am in my last quarter and in the transition period of applying to graduate school. While tying up loose ends, I am pursuing my interests in the interdisciplinary field of Linguistics and Statistics, though many know this as “natural language processing”. Current research at the CPLS lab includes the exploration and validation of the Functional Load Hypothesis and it’s relation to predicting phonological mergers.

The Functional Load Hypothesis (Jakobson, 1931; Martinet, 1952) states that sounds distinguishing more words (high functional load) are less likely to disappear or merge with other sounds during sound change (UCLA)

title: vitae
text:
Technical Skills

Languages
R, Python, SQL, HTML, SAS


Machine Learning
Tree Algorithms: XGBoost, Random Forests, Decision Trees
Regression: Logistic Regression, Linear Regression, Lasso Regression, Ridge Regression
Clustering: K-Nearest Neighbors
Deep Learning: Neural Networks, Recurrent Neural Networks, Encoder Models, Decoder Models, Encoder-Decoder Models


Computational Linguistics
Hidden Markov Models, Noisy Channel models, Large Language Models (Bert and GPT)
Working with word vectors and cosine similarity to achieve similarity scores
Observing semantic relationships
Recognizing trends within text data in order to extrapolate predictions




Education
University of California, Santa Barbara | B.S. in Statistics and Data Science, GPA: 3.5 (2021 - 2023)
Santa Barbara City College | A.A. in Liberal Arts-Science: Science & Math, GPA: 3.86 (2019 - 2021)


Projects

Undergraduate Research Assistant
UC Santa Barbara (2023 - Present)
As an undergraduate research assistant, I worked to validate the Functional Load Hypothesis – the theory that sounds distinguishing more words are less likely to disappear from a language or merge with other sounds. Using Word2Vec embeddings trained on hand-picked and preprocessed corpora, we are able to quantify the extent to which words differing by one sound, or minimal pairs, may be confused with each other in context and compared to a simple count of words. The goal of this comparison is to understand if the rate of confusibility is dependent on the particular sound the minimal pairs share.


Researcher
Evidation Health, Inc. (2023)
A collaboration with Evidation to investigate alternative diagnostic metrics for respiratory viral infections (RVI). Using remotely collected data such as an individual’s resting heart rate and caloric expenditure, we predicted the outcome of the participant’s RVI lab test result. The resulting boosted tree model observed deviations from individual participant baselines, indicating the occurrence or absence of RVI without the need for a lab test.
Abstract


Independent Researcher
UC Santa Barbara (2023)
Using synonym replacement and text summarization, we explore the methodology of medical transcription simplification and what it means for text to be “simplified”. The synonym simplification process utilizes BioBERT embeddings to identify complex terms and replace them with simpler definitions. The resulting text is then summarized using a BERT2BERT model fine-tuned on a CNN/Dailymail dataset. From the results, we use a standard method for generating reading levels, the Flesch reading ease score test, and find an overall increase in readability after applying our models
Abstract




Awards
HDR DSC Award #1924205 and Award #1924008, issued by the National Science Foundation’s Harnessing the Data Revolution Data Science Corps

title: Evidation Research
text:
I was the spokesperson and meeting-organizer for a team of five students, two industry sponsors, and an academic adviser. Learned methods for working with time-series health data to establish trends as well as determine how to handle missing data with the use of data imputation techniques in R and
Python. Developed two innovative models to define respiratory illness case onset and recovery period.

title: Yelp Research
